1:
"James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, to the twelve tribes which are scattered abroad, greeting." The witnesses note the writer, James, addressing the scattered twelve tribes. On "James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the author's self-description. Barnes and Robertson note that the writer (commonly identified as James the Just, the Lord's brother and head of the Jerusalem church) calls himself "a servant (doulos — bondservant) of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ" — a title of humble devotion, not asserting his apostleship or kinship to Jesus but his servanthood; coupling God and the Lord Jesus marks Christ's dignity.

On "to the twelve tribes which are scattered abroad," the witnesses note the recipients. Barnes, Henry, Clarke, and Ellicott agree the readers are "the twelve tribes which are scattered abroad (in the dispersion, the diaspora)" — the Jewish people scattered among the nations, specifically those of Jewish origin who had embraced the Christian faith; James writes to Jewish believers of the dispersion.

On "greeting," the grammatical-historical note the salutation: "greeting (chairein — the common Greek epistolary greeting)" — the same form James used in the Jerusalem council letter (Acts 15:23), a small mark of authenticity. The verse displays the writer, James, addressing the scattered twelve tribes.

The witnesses agree the substance: James, a humble servant of God and the Lord Jesus Christ (the Lord's brother, head of the Jerusalem church), writes to the twelve tribes of the dispersion — the scattered Jewish believers in Christ — with the customary greeting (Barnes, Robertson, Henry, Clarke, Ellicott). The verse displays the address. The verse marks the author: James, a servant. The verse displays the Lord: God and Jesus Christ. The verse displays the readers: the twelve tribes. The verse displays the scattering: in the dispersion. The salutation displays James writing to the scattered Jewish believers. The verse displays the servant: James. The verse displays the tribes: twelve. The verse displays the greeting: given. The writer, James, addressing the scattered twelve tribes, displays the opening of the epistle: James, calling himself simply a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, writes to the twelve tribes scattered abroad — the dispersed Jewish Christians — with the customary greeting. The verse displays the address, opening the call to count trials as joy (v.2).
2:
"My brethren, count it all joy when ye fall into divers temptations;" The witnesses note the call to count trials all joy. On "count it all joy," the grammatical voices note the paradoxical command. Barnes and Robertson note the striking exhortation: "count it (reckon, regard it) all joy (pure, complete joy) when ye fall into (encounter, are surrounded by) divers (poikilois — various, manifold) temptations" — believers are to regard their trials, of every kind, as occasion for the fullest joy.

On "divers temptations," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Calvin note that "temptations (peirasmois)" here means not solicitations to sin but trials, afflictions, testings — the various outward troubles and persecutions that prove faith; the word will shift to mean solicitation to evil later (v.13).

On the joy in trial, the eastern Cyril notes the reason: the believer counts trial joy not for the pain itself but for its fruit — the testing of faith works patience (v.3); knowing the good that trials produce, the Christian can rejoice even in hardship. The verse displays the call to count trials all joy.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the paradoxical command — "count it (reckon) all joy (full joy) when ye fall into divers (various) temptations," regarding trials of every kind as occasion for joy (Barnes, Robertson); "temptations (peirasmois)" meaning trials, afflictions, testings, not solicitations to sin (Barnes, Calvin); the joy grounded not in the pain but in its fruit, the testing working patience (Cyril). The verse displays the joyful trial. The verse marks the reckoning: all joy. The verse displays the trials: divers. The verse displays the meaning: testings, not enticements. The verse displays the ground: their fruit. The command displays trials counted joy for their good fruit. The verse displays the joy: full. The verse displays the temptations: various. The verse displays the brethren: addressed. The call to count trials all joy displays the bracing opening of the epistle: regard it as pure joy when you encounter trials of every kind — not because the trials are pleasant, but because, as the next verse shows, they prove and strengthen faith. The verse displays the joy in trial, grounded in the patience it works (v.3).
3:
"Knowing this, that the trying of your faith worketh patience." The brief verse notes that the trying of faith works patience. The grammatical voices note the ground of the joy. Barnes and Robertson note the reason trials are joy: "knowing this, that the trying (dokimion — the testing, proving, as metal is assayed) of your faith worketh (produces, brings about) patience (hypomonen — steadfast endurance)" — the believer knows that the testing of faith yields endurance; this knowledge is the ground of the joy commanded in v.2.

On "the trying of your faith," the witnesses note the assaying. Barnes and Calvin note that "the trying (testing)" is the process by which faith is proved genuine, as gold is tested by fire; trials assay faith, showing it real and purifying it.

On "worketh patience," the grammatical-historical note the fruit: the testing "worketh patience" — produces steadfast endurance, the habit of patiently bearing up under trial; the very fruit that makes trials valuable. The verse displays that the trying of faith works patience.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground of joy — "knowing this, that the trying (testing, proving) of your faith worketh (produces) patience (steadfast endurance)," the believer knowing that tested faith yields endurance (Barnes, Robertson); "the trying" the assaying of faith as gold by fire, proving it genuine (Barnes, Calvin); "worketh patience," producing the habit of patient endurance, the valuable fruit (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fruit of testing. The verse marks the trying: of faith. The verse displays the process: assaying. The verse displays the fruit: patience. The verse displays the knowledge: ground of joy. The testing displays faith proved and patience produced. The verse displays the trying: of faith. The verse displays the working: patience. The verse displays the endurance: gained. That the trying of faith works patience displays the reason trials are counted joy: the believer knows that the testing of his faith — like the assaying of gold — produces steadfast endurance; this fruit makes the trial worth rejoicing in. The verse displays the patience-working trial, which must be allowed its perfect work (v.4).
4:
"But let patience have her perfect work, that ye may be perfect and entire, wanting nothing." The brief verse notes the call to let patience complete its work. The grammatical voices note the perfecting. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation: "let patience have her perfect work (let endurance complete its operation, carry its work to full maturity), that ye may be perfect (teleioi — mature, complete) and entire (holoklhroi — whole, sound in every part), wanting (lacking) nothing" — patience must be allowed to finish its work, so that the believer becomes fully mature and complete.

On "perfect and entire," the witnesses note the maturity. Barnes and Calvin note the twofold aim: "perfect (mature, fully developed) and entire (whole, lacking no part)" — the goal is complete Christian character, sound and entire, deficient in nothing; the full-grown, well-rounded believer.

On "wanting nothing," the grammatical-historical note the completeness: "wanting nothing (lacking in no respect)" — the perfecting work of patience leaves the believer deficient in no grace; the fullness of Christian maturity is the goal of the trial patiently borne. The verse displays the call to let patience complete its work.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the perfecting — "let patience have her perfect work (let endurance complete its operation), that ye may be perfect (mature) and entire (whole)," patience finishing its work to bring full maturity (Barnes, Robertson); "perfect and entire," complete character, sound and lacking no part (Barnes, Calvin); "wanting nothing," deficient in no grace, the fullness of maturity (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the perfecting work. The verse marks the patience: perfect work. The verse displays the aim: perfect and entire. The verse displays the maturity: complete. The verse displays the completeness: wanting nothing. The exhortation displays patience perfecting the believer. The verse displays the patience: completed. The verse displays the perfection: attained. The verse displays the lacking: nothing. The call to let patience complete its work displays the goal of patient endurance: allow patience to finish its operation, that you may be mature and whole, deficient in nothing — the full Christian character that the trial, patiently borne, is meant to produce. The verse displays the perfecting patience, which, where wisdom is lacking, sends us to God in prayer (v.5).
5:
"If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not; and it shall be given him." The witnesses note the promise of wisdom to those who ask God. On "if any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God," the grammatical voices note the resort to prayer. Barnes and Robertson note the remedy for deficiency: "if any of you lack (is deficient in) wisdom (the practical wisdom to bear trials rightly, to understand God's dealings), let him ask of God" — the believer who lacks wisdom (especially to profit from trials) is to ask it from God in prayer.

On "that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not," the witnesses note God's generosity. Barnes and Calvin note the encouragement: God "giveth to all (all who ask) liberally (haplos — simply, generously, freely, without grudging), and upbraideth not (does not reproach, does not scold for asking or for past faults)" — God gives freely and without rebuke, unlike grudging human givers.

On "it shall be given him," the eastern Cyril notes the certainty: "it shall be given him" — the promise is sure; the God who gives generously and without reproach will surely grant the wisdom asked; the believer may pray with confidence. The verse displays the promise of wisdom to those who ask God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the remedy — "if any lack wisdom (to bear trials rightly), let him ask of God," the deficient asking wisdom in prayer (Barnes, Robertson); "giveth to all liberally (freely, generously), and upbraideth not (without reproach)," God giving freely and without rebuke, unlike grudging men (Barnes, Calvin); "it shall be given him," the sure promise, prayed with confidence (Cyril). The verse displays the promise of wisdom. The verse marks the lack: of wisdom. The verse displays the resort: ask of God. The verse displays the giver: liberal, no upbraiding. The verse displays the promise: it shall be given. The promise displays God freely granting wisdom to the asker. The verse displays the wisdom: asked. The verse displays the God: generous. The verse displays the giving: assured. The promise of wisdom to those who ask God displays the encouragement to prayer: whoever lacks the wisdom to bear trials rightly may ask it of God, who gives to all freely and without reproach — and it shall be given. The verse displays the generous God, who must be asked in faith (v.6).
6:
"But let him ask in faith, nothing wavering. For he that wavereth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed." The witnesses note the condition: asking in faith, not wavering. On "let him ask in faith, nothing wavering," the grammatical voices note the requirement. Barnes and Robertson note the condition of the promise: "let him ask in faith (believing, trusting God's power and willingness to give), nothing wavering (diakrinomenos — not doubting, not divided, not hesitating)" — the prayer for wisdom must be offered in faith, without doubt; wavering disqualifies the asker.

On "nothing wavering," the witnesses note the steady trust. Barnes and Calvin note that "wavering" is the divided, doubting mind that hesitates between believing and disbelieving; true prayer requires a settled confidence in God, not a hesitating, uncertain spirit.

On "like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed," the grammatical-historical note the vivid figure: the doubter is "like a wave (klydon — a surge, billow) of the sea driven with the wind (by the wind) and tossed (blown about)" — the wavering soul, like a restless wave, is driven and tossed by every gust, unstable, never settled; the picture of the doubting mind. The verse displays the condition: asking in faith, not wavering.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the condition — "let him ask in faith (believing), nothing wavering (not doubting)," the prayer offered in faith without doubt (Barnes, Robertson); "wavering" the divided, hesitating mind, disqualifying the asker, true prayer requiring settled confidence (Barnes, Calvin); "like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed," the doubter restless and unstable, driven by every gust (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the asking in faith. The verse marks the faith: required. The verse displays the wavering: forbidden. The verse displays the figure: a tossed wave. The verse displays the instability: of the doubter. The condition displays faith required, doubt pictured as a tossed wave. The verse displays the asking: in faith. The verse displays the wavering: condemned. The verse displays the wave: tossed. The condition — asking in faith, not wavering — displays the requirement of effective prayer: the wisdom must be asked in faith, with no doubt; for the doubter, like a wave of the sea driven and tossed by the wind, is unstable and unsettled. The verse displays the wavering soul, which receives nothing (v.7).
7:
"For let not that man think that he shall receive any thing of the Lord." The brief verse notes that the wavering man receives nothing from the Lord. The grammatical voices note the solemn conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note the consequence of doubt: "let not that man (the waverer of v.6) think (suppose, expect) that he shall receive any thing of the Lord" — the doubting, divided man must not expect to obtain anything from God; his unbelief forfeits the answer.

On "receive any thing of the Lord," the witnesses note the forfeiture. Barnes and JFB note that the wavering man receives nothing — not the wisdom he asked, nor any blessing; doubt cuts off the supply; God answers the prayer of faith, not of the divided heart.

On the principle, the grammatical-historical note the bearing: this is no arbitrary refusal but the nature of the case — the doubter, not truly trusting God, is in no condition to receive; faith is the hand that takes God's gift, and the waverer has no such hand. The verse displays that the wavering man receives nothing from the Lord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the consequence — "let not that man (the waverer) think that he shall receive any thing of the Lord," the doubter not to expect anything from God, his unbelief forfeiting the answer (Barnes, Robertson); the wavering man receiving nothing, doubt cutting off the supply (Barnes, JFB); no arbitrary refusal but the nature of the case, faith the hand that takes God's gift (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the forfeited answer. The verse marks the man: the waverer. The verse displays the warning: receive nothing. The verse displays the source: of the Lord. The verse displays the cause: doubt. The conclusion displays the doubter receiving nothing. The verse displays the thinking: corrected. The verse displays the receiving: none. The verse displays the Lord: gives not to doubt. That the wavering man receives nothing from the Lord displays the solemn consequence of doubt: the divided, doubting man must not expect to obtain anything from God — for faith is the hand that receives, and the waverer, not truly trusting, forfeits the gift. The verse displays the empty-handed doubter, the double-minded man unstable in all his ways (v.8).
8:
"A double minded man is unstable in all his ways." The brief verse notes the double-minded man unstable in all his ways. The grammatical voices note the diagnosis. Barnes and Robertson note the description of the waverer: "a double minded man (dipsychos — two-souled, of two minds, divided in loyalty between God and the world or between faith and doubt) is unstable (akatastatos — restless, inconstant, wavering) in all his ways" — the man of divided mind is unsteady in his whole conduct, not just in prayer.

On "double minded," the witnesses note the divided soul. Barnes and Calvin note that "double minded" describes one whose heart is divided — wavering between trusting God and trusting self or the world; a soul not wholly given to God, torn between two allegiances.

On "unstable in all his ways," the grammatical-historical note the pervasive instability: such a man is "unstable in all his ways" — not merely in his praying but throughout his life; the divided heart produces an inconstant, unsettled character in everything. The verse displays the double-minded man unstable in all his ways.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the diagnosis — "a double minded man (two-souled, divided) is unstable (restless, inconstant) in all his ways," the man of divided mind unsteady in his whole conduct (Barnes, Robertson); "double minded" the divided heart, wavering between God and the world (Barnes, Calvin); "unstable in all his ways," the instability pervading his whole life, not just prayer (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the divided man. The verse marks the mind: double. The verse displays the instability: in all his ways. The verse displays the division: between God and world. The verse displays the scope: his whole life. The diagnosis displays the divided heart producing pervasive instability. The verse displays the double-minded: described. The verse displays the unstable: in all. The verse displays the ways: inconstant. The double-minded man unstable in all his ways displays the deeper diagnosis of the waverer: the man of divided mind — torn between trusting God and trusting the world — is inconstant not only in prayer but throughout his whole conduct; his divided heart unsettles all he does. The verse displays the unstable double-minded man, contrasted with the rejoicing of the lowly brother (v.9).
9:
"Let the brother of low degree rejoice in that he is exalted:" The brief verse notes the lowly brother rejoicing in his exaltation. The grammatical voices note the paradox. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation to the poor believer: "let the brother of low degree (the poor, humble, lowly in worldly station) rejoice (boast, glory) in that he is exalted (in his high spiritual station, his exaltation in Christ)" — the lowly believer is to rejoice in the spiritual dignity that is his in Christ, however poor his earthly lot.

On "the brother of low degree," the witnesses note the poor Christian. Barnes and Calvin note that "of low degree" means lowly in worldly rank or poverty; many early Christians were poor; James addresses their condition with comfort.

On "rejoice in that he is exalted," the grammatical-historical note the spiritual exaltation: the poor brother "is exalted" — raised to high dignity as a child of God, an heir of the kingdom; in Christ the lowly are lifted up; he may glory not in wealth but in his exaltation in grace. The verse displays the lowly brother rejoicing in his exaltation.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exhortation — "let the brother of low degree (the poor, lowly) rejoice (glory) in that he is exalted (in his spiritual dignity in Christ)," the lowly believer rejoicing in his high station in Christ (Barnes, Robertson); "of low degree" meaning poor or humble in worldly rank (Barnes, Calvin); "exalted," raised to dignity as a child of God and heir of the kingdom (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the lowly exalted. The verse marks the brother: of low degree. The verse displays the rejoicing: in exaltation. The verse displays the lowliness: worldly. The verse displays the exaltation: spiritual. The exhortation displays the poor believer glorying in his dignity in Christ. The verse displays the lowly: rejoicing. The verse displays the exaltation: in grace. The verse displays the brother: exalted. The lowly brother rejoicing in his exaltation displays the comfort for the poor believer: let the one of humble worldly station glory in his exaltation — his high dignity as a child of God and heir of the kingdom, raised up in Christ however lowly his earthly lot. The verse displays the exalted lowly, set against the humbled rich (vv.10-11).
10:
"But the rich, in that he is made low: because as the flower of the grass he shall pass away." The brief verse notes the rich brother glorying in being made low. The grammatical voices note the reversed glory. Barnes and Robertson note the parallel exhortation: "but the rich (the wealthy believer), in that he is made low (in his humiliation, the lowering of his earthly pride, or his learning to count earthly riches as nothing)" — the rich believer is to glory not in his wealth but in being humbled, in the abasement that teaches him the vanity of riches.

On "in that he is made low," interpreters note the sense. Barnes and Calvin note that the rich man's "glory" is in his humiliation — whether his being brought low by trial, or his Christian lowliness of mind that no longer trusts in riches; he glories in what the world would count loss.

On "as the flower of the grass he shall pass away," the grammatical-historical note the reason: "as the flower of the grass (the wild flower) he shall pass away (perish, fade)" — the rich man, and his riches, are as fleeting as a flower; his wealth and even his life quickly fade; so he should glory not in riches but in his lowliness. The verse displays the rich brother glorying in being made low.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reversed glory — "the rich, in that he is made low (in his humiliation, the abasement of earthly pride)," the rich believer glorying not in wealth but in being humbled (Barnes, Robertson); the "glory" in humiliation, learning the vanity of riches (Barnes, Calvin); "as the flower of the grass he shall pass away," riches and life fleeting as a flower (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the humbled rich. The verse marks the rich: made low. The verse displays the glory: in humiliation. The verse displays the figure: the fading flower. The verse displays the reason: riches pass. The exhortation displays the rich glorying in lowliness, not wealth. The verse displays the rich: humbled. The verse displays the flower: fading. The verse displays the passing: away. The rich brother glorying in being made low displays the reversal of worldly values: the wealthy believer is to glory not in riches but in his humiliation — for he, like the flower of the grass, will pass away; his wealth and life are fleeting. The verse displays the fading rich, whose perishing is now pictured (v.11).
11:
"For the sun is no sooner risen with a burning heat, but it withereth the grass, and the flower thereof falleth, and the grace of the fashion of it perisheth: so also shall the rich man fade away in his ways." The witnesses note the rich man fading like the scorched flower. On "the sun is no sooner risen with a burning heat, but it withereth the grass," the grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note the vivid picture: "the sun is no sooner risen with a burning heat (the scorching east wind, the kauson), but it withereth the grass, and the flower thereof falleth" — the rising sun with its burning heat quickly withers the grass and makes its flower drop; the swift destruction of fragile beauty.

On "the grace of the fashion of it perisheth," the witnesses note the lost beauty. Barnes and Calvin note that "the grace (beauty, comeliness) of the fashion (appearance, form) of it perisheth" — all the flower's beauty is destroyed; what was lovely is suddenly gone; the figure of how quickly outward glory fades.

On "so also shall the rich man fade away in his ways," the grammatical-historical note the application: "so also shall the rich man fade away (wither, perish) in his ways (in his pursuits, his goings, his very course of life)" — the rich man, busy in his affairs, will wither like the flower; his wealth and life fade even as he pursues them; the warning against trusting in riches. The verse displays the rich man fading like the scorched flower.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure — "the sun is no sooner risen with a burning heat (scorching), but it withereth the grass, and the flower thereof falleth," the rising sun swiftly withering the grass and dropping its flower (Barnes, Robertson); "the grace (beauty) of the fashion (form) of it perisheth," all its loveliness suddenly destroyed (Barnes, Calvin); "so also shall the rich man fade away in his ways (his pursuits)," the rich man withering like the flower amid his affairs (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fading rich. The verse marks the sun: burning heat. The verse displays the grass: withered. The verse displays the beauty: perished. The verse displays the rich: faded. The figure displays the rich man perishing like the scorched flower. The verse displays the heat: scorching. The verse displays the flower: fallen. The verse displays the rich man: fading. The rich man fading like the scorched flower displays the vivid warning against trusting riches: as the burning sun withers the grass and its flower falls, all its beauty perishing, so the rich man fades away even amid his pursuits — his wealth and life as fleeting as the flower. The verse displays the perishing rich, over against the blessed endurer of trial (v.12).
12:
"Blessed is the man that endureth temptation: for when he is tried, he shall receive the crown of life, which the Lord hath promised to them that love him." The witnesses note the blessing and crown promised to the endurer of trial. On "blessed is the man that endureth temptation," the grammatical voices note the beatitude. Barnes and Robertson note the return to the theme of trials: "blessed (happy, to be congratulated) is the man that endureth (hypomenei — patiently bears up under) temptation (trial, peirasmon)" — the one who steadfastly endures testing is blessed; a beatitude on patient endurance.

On "for when he is tried, he shall receive the crown of life," the witnesses note the reward. Barnes and Calvin note that "when he is tried (dokimos — approved, having stood the test), he shall receive the crown (stephanos — the victor's wreath) of life" — having passed the trial, the believer receives the crown which is life (eternal life), the reward of the proved and faithful.

On "which the Lord hath promised to them that love him," the puritan Henry notes the promise and its recipients: the crown is "promised to them that love him" — to those who love God; love for God is the mark of the genuine believer who endures; the promise of the crown belongs to such. The verse displays the blessing and crown promised to the endurer of trial.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the beatitude — "blessed is the man that endureth (patiently bears) temptation (trial)," the steadfast endurer blessed (Barnes, Robertson); "when he is tried (approved), he shall receive the crown (victor's wreath) of life (eternal life)," the proved believer rewarded with the crown of life (Barnes, Calvin); "promised to them that love him," the crown belonging to those who love God, the mark of the genuine endurer (Henry). The verse displays the crown of life. The verse marks the blessing: on the endurer. The verse displays the trial: endured. The verse displays the reward: the crown of life. The verse displays the recipients: those who love him. The beatitude displays the proved believer crowned with life. The verse displays the enduring: blessed. The verse displays the crown: of life. The verse displays the love: of God. The blessing and crown promised to the endurer of trial display the reward that crowns patient endurance: blessed is the one who bears up under testing, for having stood the test he receives the crown of life — promised to those who love God. The verse displays the crown of life, set against the warning that God tempts none to evil (v.13).
13:
"Let no man say when he is tempted, I am tempted of God: for God cannot be tempted with evil, neither tempteth he any man:" The witnesses note that God tempts no man to evil. On "let no man say when he is tempted, I am tempted of God," the grammatical voices note the forbidden excuse. Barnes and Robertson note the shift in the word "tempted": "let no man say when he is tempted (solicited to sin — now in the sense of enticement to evil, not mere trial), I am tempted of (from) God" — no one, when drawn toward sin, may blame God as the author of the solicitation; the excuse that "God tempted me" is forbidden.

On "God cannot be tempted with evil," the witnesses note God's holiness. Barnes and Calvin note the reason: "God cannot be tempted with (by) evil (apeirastos — incapable of being solicited by evil, untemptable)" — God himself is wholly removed from evil, neither susceptible to it nor having any part in it; he cannot be the source of solicitation to sin.

On "neither tempteth he any man," the grammatical-historical note the distinction (reconciling with God's "tempting" Abraham, Genesis 22): God "tempteth (solicits to evil) no man" — he tests and tries men (trials, providential testing, are from him), but he never entices anyone to sin; the trial that proves faith is from God, but the solicitation to evil is never his doing. The verse displays that God tempts no man to evil.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the forbidden excuse — "let no man say... I am tempted of (from) God," none blaming God as the author of solicitation to sin (Barnes, Robertson); "God cannot be tempted with evil (untemptable)," God wholly removed from evil, no source of sin (Barnes, Calvin); "neither tempteth he any man," God testing men by trials but never enticing to sin, the trial his but not the solicitation (grammatical-historical, distinguishing peirasmos as trial from enticement). The verse displays God's purity from evil. The verse marks the excuse: forbidden. The verse displays the untemptable: God. The verse displays the distinction: trial not enticement. The verse displays the source: not God. The teaching displays God free from all part in solicitation to sin. The verse displays the saying: forbidden. The verse displays the God: untemptable. The verse displays the tempting: not his. That God tempts no man to evil displays the safeguard against blaming God: let none, when enticed to sin, say "I am tempted of God" — for God, untemptable by evil and wholly removed from it, solicits no one to sin; though he may test and try, he never entices to evil. The verse displays the sinless God, the true source of temptation being our own lust (v.14).
14:
"But every man is tempted, when he is drawn away of his own lust, and enticed." The brief verse notes that temptation springs from a man's own lust. The grammatical voices note the true source. Barnes and Robertson note the real origin of solicitation to sin: "every man is tempted, when he is drawn away (exelkomenos — dragged off, lured away, as by a baited hook) of his own lust (epithymias — desire, craving), and enticed (deleazomenos — baited, allured, as a fish to bait)" — temptation arises from within, from a man's own corrupt desire, which lures and baits him toward sin.

On "drawn away of his own lust," the witnesses note the inward enemy. Barnes and Calvin note that the source is "his own lust" — not God, not merely the devil or the world, but the man's own desire; the inward craving is what draws him; each is tempted by his own appetite.

On "drawn away... and enticed," the grammatical-historical note the imagery of the hunt and the hook: "drawn away (lured from safety) and enticed (baited)" — the language of hunting and fishing; lust acts like a hunter luring prey or a baited hook drawing a fish; the sinner is caught by his own desire. The verse displays that temptation springs from a man's own lust.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the true source — "every man is tempted, when he is drawn away (lured) of his own lust (desire), and enticed (baited)," temptation arising from a man's own corrupt desire (Barnes, Robertson); the source "his own lust," not God but the man's own craving (Barnes, Calvin); "drawn away and enticed," the imagery of the hunter's lure and the baited hook (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the inward source. The verse marks the tempting: from within. The verse displays the lure: his own lust. The verse displays the enticing: baited. The verse displays the imagery: hook and hunt. The teaching displays sin springing from one's own desire. The verse displays the drawing: away. The verse displays the lust: one's own. The verse displays the enticing: of self. That temptation springs from a man's own lust displays the true origin of sin's solicitation: every man is tempted when his own desire drags him away and baits him — like a fish lured to a hook; the source is not God but the corrupt craving within. The verse displays the inward lust, whose deadly progeny is now traced (v.15).
15:
"Then when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin: and sin, when it is finished, bringeth forth death." The witnesses note the deadly genealogy: lust conceives sin, sin brings forth death. On "when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin," the grammatical voices note the birth of sin. Barnes and Robertson note the figure of conception and birth: "when lust (desire) hath conceived (sullabousa — become pregnant), it bringeth forth (gives birth to) sin" — desire, when yielded to, conceives and gives birth to actual sin; the inward craving, embraced, becomes the outward act.

On "sin, when it is finished, bringeth forth death," the witnesses note the deadly offspring. Barnes and Calvin note the final fruit: "sin, when it is finished (apotelestheisa — full-grown, completed, brought to maturity), bringeth forth death" — sin, when it has run its full course and matured, produces death; spiritual and eternal death is the offspring of completed sin.

On the progression, the grammatical-historical note the chain: lust → sin → death; the deadly genealogy traces the course from inward desire, through its conception into act, to its final issue in death; each step begets the next, showing the fatal end of yielding to lust. The verse displays the deadly genealogy: lust conceives sin, sin brings forth death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the birth of sin — "when lust (desire) hath conceived (become pregnant), it bringeth forth (gives birth to) sin," desire yielded to conceiving and birthing actual sin (Barnes, Robertson); "sin, when it is finished (full-grown, matured), bringeth forth death," completed sin producing death (Barnes, Calvin); the chain lust → sin → death, the deadly genealogy of yielding to desire (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the genealogy of death. The verse marks the lust: conceives. The verse displays the sin: born. The verse displays the maturing: sin finished. The verse displays the offspring: death. The progression displays desire begetting sin and sin begetting death. The verse displays the lust: pregnant. The verse displays the sin: brought forth. The verse displays the death: the end. The deadly genealogy — lust conceives sin, sin brings forth death — displays the fatal course of yielding to desire: lust, when conceived, gives birth to sin; and sin, when full-grown, gives birth to death; each step begets the next, tracing the path from inward craving to fatal end. The verse displays the offspring of lust, against which the readers are warned not to err (v.16).
16:
"Do not err, my beloved brethren." The brief verse notes the warning not to be deceived. The grammatical voices note the affectionate caution. Barnes and Robertson note the transition: "do not err (be not deceived, do not be led astray), my beloved brethren" — a tender warning against the error just refuted (that God is the author of temptation, vv.13-15) and an introduction to the truth that follows (that God is the giver of all good, v.17).

On "do not err," the witnesses note the danger of deception. Barnes and Calvin note that the warning guards against a fundamental error about God's character — supposing that evil comes from him; the readers are not to be misled on this vital point.

On "my beloved brethren," the puritan Henry notes the affection: the tender address "my beloved brethren" softens the warning with love; James corrects not coldly but as one who loves them, earnest that they not be deceived about God. The verse displays the warning not to be deceived.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the caution — "do not err (be not deceived, led astray), my beloved brethren," a tender warning against the error refuted and introducing the truth following (Barnes, Robertson); the warning guarding against a fundamental error about God's character, that evil comes from him (Barnes, Calvin); "my beloved brethren," the affectionate address softening the warning (Henry). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the caution: do not err. The verse displays the danger: deception. The verse displays the affection: beloved brethren. The verse displays the hinge: between error and truth. The warning displays the readers cautioned against deception about God. The verse displays the erring: forbidden. The verse displays the brethren: beloved. The verse displays the warning: tender. The warning not to be deceived displays the affectionate hinge of the argument: "do not err, my beloved brethren" — be not led astray about God's character, supposing evil comes from him; rather, as the next verse shows, every good gift comes down from him. The verse displays the caution against error, answered by the truth of God's good giving (v.17).
17:
"Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and cometh down from the Father of lights, with whom is no variableness, neither shadow of turning." The witnesses note that every good gift comes from the unchanging Father of lights. On "every good gift and every perfect gift is from above," the grammatical voices note the source of all good. Barnes and Robertson note the truth answering the error: "every good gift (dosis — the act of giving, every good giving) and every perfect gift (dorema — the gift bestowed) is from above (from heaven)" — all good, of every kind, comes down from above; God is the author of good, not evil.

On "cometh down from the Father of lights," the witnesses note the title of God. Barnes and Calvin note that good gifts come "from the Father of lights (the Creator and source of all lights — the heavenly luminaries, and so of all light, material and spiritual)" — God, who made and governs the lights of heaven, is the Father and fountain of all illumination and good.

On "with whom is no variableness, neither shadow of turning," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the unchanging constancy: unlike the lights of heaven (the sun and stars that rise, set, and shift), with God is "no variableness (parallage — change, variation), neither shadow of turning (trope — no shadow cast by turning, no eclipse or alteration)" — God is utterly unchanging in his goodness; the heavenly bodies vary and cast shifting shadows, but the Father of lights never changes; his goodness is constant. The verse displays that every good gift comes from the unchanging Father of lights.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the source — "every good gift (giving) and every perfect gift (bestowed) is from above," all good coming down from heaven, God the author of good (Barnes, Robertson); "the Father of lights," the Creator of the heavenly luminaries, fountain of all light and good (Barnes, Calvin); "no variableness, neither shadow of turning," God unchanging in goodness, unlike the shifting heavenly lights (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the unchanging Giver. The verse marks the gifts: good and perfect. The verse displays the source: from above. The verse displays the title: Father of lights. The verse displays the constancy: no variableness. The truth displays all good flowing from the unchanging God. The verse displays the gift: good. The verse displays the Father: of lights. The verse displays the changelessness: of God. That every good gift comes from the unchanging Father of lights displays the truth that answers the error about God: all good of every kind comes down from above, from the Father of lights — who, unlike the shifting sun and stars, has no variableness nor shadow of turning, but is unchangeably good. The verse displays the unchanging God, who of his own will begat us (v.18).
18:
"Of his own will begat he us with the word of truth, that we should be a kind of firstfruits of his creatures." The witnesses note the new birth by God's will through the word of truth. On "of his own will begat he us," the grammatical voices note the sovereign act of regeneration. Barnes and Robertson note the chief instance of God's good gift: "of his own will (boulctheis — having willed it, by his own free purpose and choice) begat he us (apekyesen — gave us birth, regenerated us)" — God, of his own free will, gave us spiritual birth; the new birth is the supreme example of his good giving, springing from his sovereign good pleasure.

On "with the word of truth," the witnesses note the instrument. Barnes and Calvin note that God begat us "with (by means of) the word of truth (the gospel, the message of truth)" — the new birth is wrought through the instrumentality of the gospel; the word of truth is the means God uses to regenerate.

On "that we should be a kind of firstfruits of his creatures," the grammatical-historical note the purpose: "that we should be a kind of firstfruits (aparche — the first and choicest portion, consecrated to God) of his creatures (his creation)" — believers, regenerated, are as the firstfruits of God's new creation, the first and consecrated portion, a pledge of the larger harvest to come. The verse displays the new birth by God's will through the word of truth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sovereign act — "of his own will (by his free purpose) begat he us (regenerated us)," God giving spiritual birth of his own free will, the supreme good gift (Barnes, Robertson); "with the word of truth (the gospel)," the new birth wrought through the gospel as instrument (Barnes, Calvin); "a kind of firstfruits (first and choicest portion) of his creatures," believers the firstfruits of God's new creation, a pledge of the harvest (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the new birth. The verse marks the will: God's own. The verse displays the begetting: regeneration. The verse displays the means: the word of truth. The verse displays the purpose: firstfruits. The act displays God regenerating us by his will through the word. The verse displays the will: free. The verse displays the word: of truth. The verse displays the firstfruits: consecrated. The new birth by God's will through the word of truth displays the crown of God's good giving: of his own free will he gave us spiritual birth through the word of truth, that we should be a kind of firstfruits of his creatures — the first and consecrated portion of his new creation. The verse displays the regenerating word, which calls for swift hearing and slow speech (v.19).
19:
"Wherefore, my beloved brethren, let every man be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath:" The witnesses note the threefold counsel: swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath. On "let every man be swift to hear," the grammatical voices note the first counsel. Barnes and Robertson note the practical wisdom flowing from the word of truth (v.18): "let every man be swift to hear (quick, ready to listen — especially to the word of God, and to instruction)" — the believer should be eager to hear, ready to receive truth and counsel.

On "slow to speak," the witnesses note the second counsel. Barnes and Calvin note "slow to speak (not hasty, not rash with the tongue)" — restraint in speaking; not quick to talk, to teach, or to assert oneself; the curbing of the tongue (a major theme of the epistle).

On "slow to wrath," the puritan Henry notes the third: "slow to wrath (slow to anger, not easily provoked)" — restraint of temper; the believer is not to be quick-tempered, especially in receiving the word or in dispute; the reason follows (v.20), that human wrath does not work God's righteousness. The verse displays the threefold counsel: swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the threefold wisdom — "swift to hear (ready to listen, especially to the word)," eager to receive truth (Barnes, Robertson); "slow to speak (not hasty with the tongue)," restraint in speaking (Barnes, Calvin); "slow to wrath (slow to anger)," restraint of temper, not easily provoked (Henry). The verse displays the counsel. The verse marks the hearing: swift. The verse displays the speaking: slow. The verse displays the wrath: slow. The verse displays the wisdom: practical. The counsel displays the believer quick to hear, restrained in speech and anger. The verse displays the swift: to hear. The verse displays the slow: to speak. The verse displays the slow: to wrath. The threefold counsel — swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath — displays the practical wisdom flowing from the regenerating word: every believer should be quick to listen and receive truth, restrained in speech, and slow to anger. The verse displays the counsel of restraint, grounded in the uselessness of human wrath (v.20).
20:
"For the wrath of man worketh not the righteousness of God." The brief verse notes that human wrath does not work God's righteousness. The grammatical voices note the reason for slowness to anger. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of v.19's counsel: "the wrath (anger, the angry temper) of man worketh not (does not produce, does not accomplish) the righteousness of God (the righteousness God requires and approves)" — human anger does not bring about the righteous conduct or character that God desires; passion does not serve God's holy ends.

On "the wrath of man," the witnesses note the human temper. Barnes and Calvin note that "the wrath of man" is the angry, passionate spirit native to fallen men; it is restless and destructive, not productive of holiness; far from advancing God's cause, it hinders it.

On "worketh not the righteousness of God," the grammatical-historical note the failure: human wrath "worketh not the righteousness of God" — it neither produces personal righteousness nor furthers God's righteous purposes; rather it breeds strife and sin; hence the believer is to be slow to wrath (v.19). The verse displays that human wrath does not work God's righteousness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground — "the wrath (anger) of man worketh not (does not produce) the righteousness of God," human anger not accomplishing the righteousness God requires (Barnes, Robertson); "the wrath of man" the passionate fallen temper, restless and destructive, not productive of holiness (Barnes, Calvin); "worketh not the righteousness of God," neither producing personal righteousness nor furthering God's purposes, the reason to be slow to wrath (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the uselessness of wrath. The verse marks the wrath: of man. The verse displays the failure: works not righteousness. The verse displays the righteousness: of God. The verse displays the lesson: be slow to wrath. The teaching displays human anger failing God's righteous ends. The verse displays the wrath: barren. The verse displays the righteousness: unattained. The verse displays the man: passionate. That human wrath does not work God's righteousness displays the reason for the counsel to be slow to anger: the wrath of man does not produce the righteousness God requires or further his holy ends — passion breeds strife, not holiness; therefore the believer restrains his anger. The verse displays the barren wrath, calling for meek reception of the word (v.21).
21:
"Wherefore lay apart all filthiness and superfluity of naughtiness, and receive with meekness the engrafted word, which is able to save your souls." The witnesses note the call to put away evil and receive the engrafted word. On "lay apart all filthiness and superfluity of naughtiness," the grammatical voices note the stripping off. Barnes and Robertson note the negative command: "lay apart (put off, strip away, as filthy garments) all filthiness (rhyparian — moral defilement, uncleanness) and superfluity of naughtiness (the overflow, excess, of malice or wickedness)" — the believer is to cast off all moral filth and the abundance of wickedness, as one strips off dirty clothing.

On "receive with meekness the engrafted word," the witnesses note the positive command. Barnes and Calvin note: "receive (welcome) with meekness (gentleness, a teachable, submissive spirit) the engrafted (emphyton — implanted, ingrafted) word" — the word of the gospel, implanted in the heart, is to be received meekly; not with the wrath just condemned (v.20) but with a humble, docile spirit.

On "which is able to save your souls," the grammatical-historical note the saving power: the engrafted word "is able to save your souls" — the implanted word has saving power; received and obeyed, it brings the salvation of the soul; the gospel is the means of salvation. The verse displays the call to put away evil and receive the engrafted word.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the negative — "lay apart (strip off) all filthiness (moral defilement) and superfluity of naughtiness (excess of wickedness)," casting off all moral filth (Barnes, Robertson); "receive with meekness (a teachable spirit) the engrafted (implanted) word," welcoming the implanted gospel meekly, not with wrath (Barnes, Calvin); "able to save your souls," the implanted word having saving power, the means of salvation (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the received word. The verse marks the stripping: filthiness put off. The verse displays the excess: of wickedness. The verse displays the receiving: with meekness. The verse displays the power: to save souls. The call displays evil cast off and the saving word welcomed. The verse displays the filthiness: laid apart. The verse displays the word: engrafted. The verse displays the salvation: of souls. The call to put away evil and receive the engrafted word displays the twofold response to the gospel: strip off all moral filth and the excess of wickedness, and welcome with a meek, teachable spirit the implanted word — which is able to save your souls. The verse displays the saving word, which must be done and not merely heard (v.22).
22:
"But be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving your own selves." The witnesses note the call to be doers, not hearers only. On "be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only," the grammatical voices note the central exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note the heart of the matter: "be ye doers (poietai — practicers, those who carry out) of the word, and not hearers only (akroatai — mere listeners, as at a lecture)" — the believer must not merely listen to the word but practice it; obedience, not mere hearing, is required.

On "hearers only," the witnesses note the empty hearing. Barnes and Calvin note that to be a "hearer only" is to listen to the word without obeying it — a fatal mistake; mere hearing, without doing, profits nothing; the word must be acted upon.

On "deceiving your own selves," the puritan Henry notes the self-deception: the hearer-only is "deceiving (paralogizomenoi — deluding by false reasoning) your own selves" — he deceives himself, imagining that hearing alone makes him right with God; a dangerous self-delusion, mistaking acquaintance with the truth for obedience to it. The verse displays the call to be doers, not hearers only.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exhortation — "be ye doers (practicers) of the word, and not hearers only (mere listeners)," practicing the word, not merely listening (Barnes, Robertson); "hearers only" the empty hearing that does not obey, profiting nothing (Barnes, Calvin); "deceiving your own selves," the self-delusion of mistaking hearing for obedience (Henry). The verse displays the doing of the word. The verse marks the doers: required. The verse displays the hearers: only, condemned. The verse displays the deception: of self. The verse displays the danger: false security. The call displays obedience required, mere hearing a self-deceit. The verse displays the doing: commanded. The verse displays the hearing: insufficient. The verse displays the self: deceived. The call to be doers, not hearers only, displays the central practical demand of the epistle: be those who carry out the word, not mere listeners — for the hearer who does not obey deceives himself, mistaking acquaintance with the truth for true religion. The verse displays the doer of the word, illustrated by the man in the mirror (vv.23-24).
23:
"For if any be a hearer of the word, and not a doer, he is like unto a man beholding his natural face in a glass:" The brief verse notes the hearer-only likened to a man glancing in a mirror. The grammatical voices note the comparison. Barnes and Robertson note the figure: "if any be a hearer of the word, and not a doer, he is like unto a man beholding (katanoounti — observing, looking at) his natural face (the face he was born with) in a glass (esoptron — a mirror)" — the hearer who does not obey is like a man who looks at his own face in a mirror.

On "beholding his natural face in a glass," the witnesses note the glance. Barnes and Calvin note the point of the comparison: the man looks at his reflection — sees himself as he is, perhaps his flaws and need — but only momentarily; the mirror of the word shows him his true condition.

On the figure, the grammatical-historical note the application: the word is like a mirror that reveals a man to himself, showing his spiritual face and faults; the hearer-only glances in but does not act on what he sees — the comparison developed in v.24. The verse displays the hearer-only likened to a man glancing in a mirror.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure — "a hearer... and not a doer... is like unto a man beholding (observing) his natural face (his born face) in a glass (mirror)," the disobedient hearer like one looking at his face in a mirror (Barnes, Robertson); the man seeing himself as he is, his flaws revealed (Barnes, Calvin); the word a mirror showing a man his spiritual condition, glanced at but not acted on (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mirror figure. The verse marks the hearer: not a doer. The verse displays the likeness: a man at a mirror. The verse displays the face: his natural face. The verse displays the mirror: the word. The figure displays the word as a mirror revealing the self. The verse displays the beholding: in a glass. The verse displays the face: natural. The verse displays the hearer: glancing. The hearer-only likened to a man glancing in a mirror displays the apt comparison: the one who hears the word but does not obey is like a man looking at his own face in a mirror — the word, like a glass, shows him his true spiritual condition. The verse displays the man at the mirror, who forgets what he saw (v.24).
24:
"For he beholdeth himself, and goeth his way, and straightway forgetteth what manner of man he was." The brief verse notes the man forgetting at once what he saw. The grammatical voices note the swift forgetting. Barnes and Robertson note the completion of the figure: "he beholdeth himself (looks at his reflection), and goeth his way (departs), and straightway (immediately, at once) forgetteth what manner of man he was" — the man glances in the mirror, walks off, and instantly forgets what he saw of himself; the fleeting, ineffective look.

On "goeth his way, and straightway forgetteth," the witnesses note the lost impression. Barnes and Calvin note that the impression made by the mirror is momentary; as soon as the man turns away he forgets his own appearance; the look left no lasting effect, changed nothing.

On the application, the grammatical-historical note the point: so the hearer-only, having heard the word and seen his condition, goes away and forgets — the word makes no lasting impression, produces no change; he is none the better for having heard. The verse displays the man forgetting at once what he saw.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the swift forgetting — "he beholdeth himself, and goeth his way, and straightway (at once) forgetteth what manner of man he was," the man glancing, departing, and instantly forgetting his appearance (Barnes, Robertson); the momentary impression lost as soon as he turns away, changing nothing (Barnes, Calvin); so the hearer-only forgets the word and is none the better (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the forgotten image. The verse marks the beholding: himself. The verse displays the departing: goeth his way. The verse displays the forgetting: straightway. The verse displays the application: the word forgotten. The figure displays the fleeting look that changes nothing. The verse displays the beholding: brief. The verse displays the forgetting: immediate. The verse displays the impression: lost. The man forgetting at once what he saw displays the futility of mere hearing: as a man glances in the mirror, walks away, and instantly forgets his own face, so the hearer-only sees his condition in the word but at once forgets it, changed by nothing he heard. The verse displays the forgetful hearer, contrasted with the doer who looks into the perfect law (v.25).
25:
"But whoso looketh into the perfect law of liberty, and continueth therein, he being not a forgetful hearer, but a doer of the work, this man shall be blessed in his deed." The witnesses note the blessed doer who looks into the perfect law of liberty. On "whoso looketh into the perfect law of liberty," the grammatical voices note the gospel law. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the glancing hearer: "whoso looketh into (parakypsas — stoops down to gaze intently, peers carefully) the perfect law of liberty (the gospel, the law that is perfect and that brings freedom)" — the true believer gazes intently into the gospel, here called "the perfect law of liberty" because it is complete and frees from sin's bondage.

On "and continueth therein," the witnesses note the perseverance. Barnes and Calvin note that he "continueth therein (remains, abides in it)" — not a passing glance but an abiding attention; he keeps looking and obeying; perseverance distinguishes him from the forgetful hearer.

On "he being not a forgetful hearer, but a doer of the work, this man shall be blessed in his deed," the puritan Henry notes the blessing: "not a forgetful hearer (who forgets, v.24), but a doer of the work (one who acts on it)" — this man "shall be blessed in his deed (in his doing)" — the blessing is in the doing itself; the obedient doer, not the mere hearer, is blessed. The verse displays the blessed doer who looks into the perfect law of liberty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gospel law — "whoso looketh into (gazes intently into) the perfect law of liberty (the gospel that is complete and frees)," the believer gazing into the gospel, the perfect freeing law (Barnes, Robertson); "continueth therein (abides in it)," an abiding attention, not a passing glance, persevering (Barnes, Calvin); "not a forgetful hearer, but a doer of the work... blessed in his deed," the obedient doer blessed in the doing itself (Henry). The verse displays the blessed doer. The verse marks the looking: into the perfect law. The verse displays the liberty: the gospel frees. The verse displays the continuing: abiding. The verse displays the blessing: in the deed. The figure displays the doer blessed in obeying the gospel. The verse displays the law: of liberty. The verse displays the doer: of the work. The verse displays the blessing: in doing. The blessed doer who looks into the perfect law of liberty displays the contrast with the forgetful hearer: the one who gazes intently into the gospel — the perfect law that gives freedom — and abides in it, being not a forgetful hearer but a doer, shall be blessed in his very doing. The verse displays the blessed doer, whose true religion bridles the tongue (v.26).
26:
"If any man among you seem to be religious, and bridleth not his tongue, but deceiveth his own heart, this man's religion is vain." The witnesses note that unbridled speech makes religion vain. On "if any man among you seem to be religious, and bridleth not his tongue," the grammatical voices note the test of the tongue. Barnes and Robertson note the searching test: "if any man among you seem (thinks himself, or appears) to be religious (threskos — outwardly devout, observant of religious worship), and bridleth not (does not curb, rein in) his tongue" — one may appear devout, yet fail to control his speech; the unbridled tongue exposes the hollowness of mere outward religion.

On "but deceiveth his own heart," the witnesses note the self-deception. Barnes and Calvin note that such a man "deceiveth (deludes) his own heart" — he deceives himself, imagining his outward devotion is true religion while his ungoverned tongue betrays him; a self-delusion about his real state.

On "this man's religion is vain," the puritan Henry notes the verdict: "this man's religion (threskeia — religious observance) is vain (empty, worthless, futile)" — his religion, however outwardly observant, is worthless; an uncontrolled tongue empties profession of all value; true religion governs the tongue. The verse displays that unbridled speech makes religion vain.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the test — "if any man seem (thinks himself) to be religious (outwardly devout), and bridleth not (curbs not) his tongue," outward devotion failing to control speech (Barnes, Robertson); "deceiveth his own heart," self-deceived, mistaking outward devotion for true religion (Barnes, Calvin); "this man's religion is vain (empty, worthless)," the unbridled tongue emptying profession of value (Henry). The verse displays the vain religion. The verse marks the seeming: religious. The verse displays the failure: tongue unbridled. The verse displays the deception: of the heart. The verse displays the verdict: religion vain. The test displays the ungoverned tongue exposing empty religion. The verse displays the tongue: unbridled. The verse displays the heart: deceived. The verse displays the religion: vain. That unbridled speech makes religion vain displays the searching test of true devotion: if a man appears religious yet does not bridle his tongue, he deceives his own heart, and his religion is worthless — for an ungoverned tongue empties all outward profession of value. The verse displays the vain religion, over against the pure religion God approves (v.27).
27:
"Pure religion and undefiled before God and the Father is this, To visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction, and to keep himself unspotted from the world." The witnesses note the description of pure religion: mercy and purity. On "pure religion and undefiled before God and the Father," the grammatical voices note the standard. Barnes and Robertson note the definition: "pure (katara — clean) religion and undefiled (amiantos — unstained) before God and the Father (in God's sight, by his judgment) is this" — James defines the religion that is genuine and acceptable to God, not the whole of religion but its characteristic marks, given over against vain religion (v.26).

On "to visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction," the witnesses note the work of mercy. Barnes and Calvin note the first mark: "to visit (episkeptesthai — care for, look after, relieve) the fatherless (orphans) and widows in their affliction (distress)" — active compassion to the helpless and needy; practical love to those who cannot repay; the fruit of true religion in deeds of mercy.

On "to keep himself unspotted from the world," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the second mark, personal purity: "to keep himself unspotted (aspilon — unstained) from the world (its corruption, defilement, sinful ways)" — alongside active mercy, the believer is to keep himself pure from the world's pollution; true religion unites compassion to the needy with separation from sin. The verse displays the description of pure religion: mercy and purity.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the definition — "pure (clean) and undefiled (unstained) religion before God... is this," the marks of genuine religion acceptable to God, against vain religion (Barnes, Robertson); "to visit (care for, relieve) the fatherless and widows in their affliction," active compassion to the helpless, love in deeds (Barnes, Calvin); "to keep himself unspotted (unstained) from the world," personal purity from the world's corruption (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays pure religion. The verse marks the religion: pure and undefiled. The verse displays the judge: God the Father. The verse displays the mercy: to orphans and widows. The verse displays the purity: unspotted from the world. The definition displays true religion as mercy joined to purity. The verse displays the religion: genuine. The verse displays the visiting: of the needy. The verse displays the keeping: unspotted. The description of pure religion — mercy and purity — displays the marks of the devotion God approves: pure and undefiled religion before God is to care for orphans and widows in their distress, and to keep oneself unspotted from the world — active compassion joined to personal holiness. The verse displays pure religion, closing the chapter on trials, the word, and true devotion.
